coredata.dc:title: Coating layered double hydroxides with carbon dots for highly efficient removal of multiple dyes
coredata.dc:creator: Ge, Jiahui
Lian, Lili
Wang, Xiyue
Cao, Xueling
Gao, Wenxiu
Lou, Dawei
coredata.dc:description: Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) and layered double oxides (LDOs) are desirable adsorption materials for printing and wastewater treatment owing to their outstanding anion exchange abilities, abundant active sites, and eco-friendly nature. In this study, a versatile LDO hybrid coated with carbon dots (CDs@MgAl-LDO) was constructed by modifying sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate on the surface of MgAl-LDH as a carbon precursor, followed by ligand carbonization and hydrotalcite dehydration at 450 °C under N2 flow. CDs@MgAl-LDO displayed a hexagonal lamellar architecture with a plate lateral size of approximately 500 nm. It had a higher BET specific surface area (28.61 m2/g) than MgAl-LDO (11.48 m2/g). X-ray diffraction analysis revealed that CDs@MgAl-LDO maintained the “memory effect” of LDOs and could retrieve the original structure when dispersed in water. Moreover, the modified carbon dots change the intrinsically hydrophilic nature of LDOs and help to improve the affinity for organic contaminants, including both cationic and anionic dyes. The adsorption of dyes on CDs@MgAl-LDO followed a pseudo-second-order kinetic model with correlation coefficients (R2) ranging from 0.9901 to 0.9911 and exhibited Freundlich-type heterogeneous adsorption. It showed superior adsorption performance for three dyes, with the maximum adsorption capacity of 3628.9–5174.1 mg/g, thereby outperforming previously reported LDH-based adsorbents. This work developed a facile approach for preparing new carbon dots-LDH hybrids for the highly efficient removal of multiple dyes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Layered double hydroxide
Carbon dots
Hybrids
Adsorption
Dyes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of pH and adsorption mechanisms

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs@MgAl-LDO; (b) effect of SDBS addition and (c) pyrolysis condition on the synthesis of CDs@MgAl-LDO.

(a) XRD pattern and (b) FTIR of MgAl-LDH, SDBS@MgAl-LDH and CDs@MgAl-LDO; (c) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of MgAl-LDO and CDs@MgAl-LDO; (d, e) XPS spectra of C 1s and O 1s of CDs@MgAl-LDO; (f) the static contact angle of MgAl-LDO and CDs@MgAl-LDO.
(a, b) SEM images of MgAl-LDO, (c, d) SEM images of CDs@MgAl-LDO; (e) EDS of CDs@MgAl-LDO; (f) element mappings of CDs@MgAl-LDO; (g, h) High-resolution TEM images of CDs@MgAl-LDO.

(a) Plots of dye uptake against adsorption time on CDs@MgAl-LDO (m = 0.003 g; V = 50 mL; temperature = 25 °C); (b) the fitting plots of PS model; (d) the fitting plots of intra-particle diffusion model.

(a) Effect of initial pH on dye adsorption on CDs@MgAl-LDO (C0 = 200 mg/L, m = 0.003 g; V = 50 mL; temperature = 25 °C); (b) the surface potential of CDs@MgAl-LDO at various pH; (c) XRD patterns of CDs@MgAl-LDO before and after dye adsorption.
Schematic illumination of the adsorption mechanisms of CDs@MgAl-LDO.
Comparison of the dye adsorption capacity of CDs@MgAl-LDO with other LDH-based adsorbents reported in the literatures.

head.ce:title: Coating layered double hydroxides with carbon dots for highly efficient removal of multiple dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Jilin Institute of Chemical Technology, No. 45 Chengde Street, Jilin 132022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Jilin Institute of Chemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Jilin Institute of Chemical Technology, No. 45 Chengde Street, Jilin 132022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) and layered double oxides (LDOs) are desirable adsorption materials for printing and wastewater treatment owing to their outstanding anion exchange abilities, abundant active sites, and eco-friendly nature. In this study, a versatile LDO hybrid coated with carbon dots (CDs@MgAl-LDO) was constructed by modifying sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate on the surface of MgAl-LDH as a carbon precursor, followed by ligand carbonization and hydrotalcite dehydration at 450 °C under N2 flow. CDs@MgAl-LDO displayed a hexagonal lamellar architecture with a plate lateral size of approximately 500 nm. It had a higher BET specific surface area (28.61 m2/g) than MgAl-LDO (11.48 m2/g). X-ray diffraction analysis revealed that CDs@MgAl-LDO maintained the “memory effect” of LDOs and could retrieve the original structure when dispersed in water. Moreover, the modified carbon dots change the intrinsically hydrophilic nature of LDOs and help to improve the affinity for organic contaminants, including both cationic and anionic dyes. The adsorption of dyes on CDs@MgAl-LDO followed a pseudo-second-order kinetic model with correlation coefficients (R2) ranging from 0.9901 to 0.9911 and exhibited Freundlich-type heterogeneous adsorption. It showed superior adsorption performance for three dyes, with the maximum adsorption capacity of 3628.9–5174.1 mg/g, thereby outperforming previously reported LDH-based adsorbents. This work developed a facile approach for preparing new carbon dots-LDH hybrids for the highly efficient removal of multiple dyes.

ce:section.ce:para: Printing and dyeing wastewater is a primary contributor to aquatic ecosystem pollution because it poses threats to the health and safety of humans and aquatic organisms (Hu et al., 2021a). Therefore, printing and dyeing wastewater must be purified to a safe level before it is released into aquatic ecosystems. Among the various techniques for wastewater treatment, adsorption is the most simple, effective, and safe way to control pollution. Various nanomaterials, such as carbon-based materials (Amran and Zaini, 2021), natural biopolymers (Chang and Juang, 2005), metallic materials (Szewczuk-Karpisz et al., 2021), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) (Ma et al., 2016), covalent organic frameworks (COFs) (Wang et al., 2021), and synthetic polymeric materials (Waheed et al., 2021) have been developed for the adsorption of toxic colorants. However, metallic materials have limited adsorption capacity because of their few accessible binding sites. The adsorption of dyes on natural biopolymers, such as chitosan, largely depends on pH. Furthermore, adsorbents with a high adsorption capacity such as GO, COFs, MOFs, and synthetic polymers, require expensive raw precursors and involve complicated procedures (Liao et al., 2022).

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs), which are commonly known as hydrotalcite, are two-dimensional inorganic lamellar materials that contain exchangeable intercalated anions between hydroxide layers (Sun et al., 2020). LDH materials are potential adsorbents for toxic pollutants because they are nontoxic, they are less expensive than other materials, and they involve facile synthesis procedures. Direct calcination of LDHs at high temperatures ranging from 400 °C to 600 °C could conventionally change them to layered double oxides (LDOs) (Daud et al., 2019). LDOs have a “memory effect” and commonly reconstruct their original layered structure with existing anions and water when dispersed in aqueous solutions. Compared with pristine LDH materials, calcinated LDOs not only enhance the chemical and thermal stability but also improve the adsorption performance for object contaminants (Yao et al., 2017). However, LDH/LDO nanomaterials have a limited applications in the removal of cationic dyes and non-ionic organic contaminants (Jiang et al., 2019). Conversely, various kind of hydrotalcite-containing hybrids have been developed to expand their practical applications (Miao et al., 2021; Lyu et al., 2020; Wang and Zhang, 2021; Li et al., 2019). Among them, carbon-based materials possess sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and have large amounts of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface. Incorporating carbon-based materials into LDOs to construct new types of carbon/LDO hybrids that possess the advantageous properties of both materials is of great importance. Therefore, various carbon/LDO hybrids, such as graphene/LDOs (Zhang et al., 2021), fullerene/LDOs (Ju et al., 2017) and carbon nanohelix/LDOs (Zhang et al., 2016) have been prepared and used to remove cationic dyes, phosphate, heavy metals and antibiotics. Li et al. (2020) coated ultra-thin amorphous carbon shells onto ZnAl-LDO to prepare an amorphous carbon/LDO hybrid (ZnAl-LDO@C) via ligand and phase transformation. Compared with its counterparts (e.g., ZnAl-LDH), ZnAl-LDO@C has a faster adsorption rate and a better adsorption performance for methyl orange and [Cr(VI)] ions, and its uptake capacity to these pollutants can be as high as 1539 and 411 mg/g, respectively. Zhang et al. (2014) prepared LDH-carbon dots (CDs) via the colloidal deposition method, in which the CDs were attached onto the LDH surface by electrostatic attraction. The LDH-CDs composites showed an uptake capacity of 185 mg/g for methyl blue owing to the hydrogen-bond between LDH and methyl blue.

As a most promising class of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, CDs have attracted increased attention in electrochemistry, catalysis, biochemistry, and adsorption/separation because of their versatile architectures, low cytotoxicity, stability, and good biocompatibility. In general, CDs are usually obtained via hydrothermal and solvothermal reactions by using carbohydrates, organic acids, organic amines and biomass-containing carbon as carbon sources. However, there are two factors limit its application in the adsorption. First, CDs are difficult to scale up because of the inherent limitations of these strategies, which often suffer from low yield during preparation and require high solvent consumption in the post-treatment procedure. Second, the CDs prepared by the above methods are highly hydrophilic, which is unfavorable in the adsorption of nonpolar and weakly polar substances (Rahmanian et al., 2018). Herein, we proposed a novel approach for in-situ chemical transformation of MgAl-LDH modified by organic anions into the corresponding CDs coated MgAl-LDO hybrid (CDs/MgAl-LDO). Notably, graphitic CDs were created during pyrolysis with MgAl-LDO as substrate for the CD growth and sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS) as the carbon source, thereby avoiding time-consuming CD purification procedures. In contrast to the hybrid materials reported in previous studies, the CDs modified on LDOs was hydrophobic. Thus, it offers a unique opportunity for improving the adsorption performance for both anionic and cationic dyes. Furthermore, the structure and surface properties of CDs/MgAl-LDOs were evaluated via electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD), and Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The roles of CDs in CDs/MgAl-LDO were evaluated by comparing the adsorption performance of the hybrid with that of the pristine LDH. The vital factors that influence the adsorption property of CDs/MgAl-LDO were identified and the adsorption mechanisms were explored.

Analytical grade Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, malachite green (MG), direct blue 5 (DB5), congo red (CR), and urea were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Analytical grade SDBS was bought from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China).

The SDBS-LDH precursor (SDBS@MgAl-LDH) was synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method. In brief, 1.6 mmol of Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.5 mmol of Al(NO3)3·9H2O and 8 mmol of urea were added to 80 mL ultrapure water in a beaker and then sonicated for 20 min to form a uniform solution. Then, SDBS was weighed and dissolved in the above solution. The solution was magnetically stirred for 30 min and then transferred into a 100 mL autoclave, reacted at 150 °C for 6 h, and cooled down to natural temperature. The material obtained was thoroughly washed with ultrapure water and ethanol and then dried at 60 °C for 6 h to obtain SDBS@MgAl-LDH. SDBS@MgAl-LDH was calcined in a tube furnace at 450 °C for 2 h under N2 atmosphere to obtain the desired off-white LDO composite coated with CDs (CDs@MgAl-LDO). The conditions for preparing CDs@MgAl-LDO were evaluated. The samples were obtained by changing the amount of SDBS added (0–20 mmol) and then activating them at 450 °C. Samples of CDs@MgAl-LDO350 (calcined at 350 °C), CDs@MgAl-LDO550 (calcined at 550 °C), and CDs@MgAl-LDO450M (calcined in a muffle furnace at 450 °C) were prepared in the same manner but under different calcination conditions. For comparison, MgAl-LDO was also prepared under the same conditions but without adding SDBS during the hydrothermal procedure.

The morphology of CDs@MgAl-LDO was analyzed via HRTEM (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA) and SEM (JEOL-JSM-6490LV, Japan) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, X-Max; Oxford University, UK). The infrared spectrum of the prepared materials was recorded within the range of 4000–400 cm−1 by using an FTIR spectrophotometer (Vertex 80 V, Bruker, Germany). The crystalline phase of CDs@MgAl-LDO and the relative LDHs were recorded via XRD (D8 Advanced, Bruker, Germany) with Cu-Kα radiation within the 2θ range of 3–90°. The surface charges of the products were measured on a ZS/ZEN3690 Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern, England). The concentrations of the three dyes (i.e., MG, DB5, and CR) were measured using a Puxi (TU-1240) spectrophotometer.

Batch adsorption experiments were performed to evaluate the adsorption performance of CDs@MgAl-LDO for the three dyes under different conditions. In brief, 3 mg of CDs@MgAl-LDO was added to 50 mL of dye solution with an initial concentration of 200–400 mg/L. The mixed solution was shaken at 298 K for a fixed time. After centrifugation separation, the concentration of residual dye was measured using a spectrophotometer. The parameters vital to dye adsorption, including pH, time and concentration, were evaluated. The experimental data were then fitted using the typical Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models. Adsorption capacity (q
t , mg/g) at time t was calculated as follows:
(1)
q
t
=
( C
0
−
C
t
) × V
m
where C
0 (mg/L) is the initial dye concentration, C
t (mg/L) is the final dye concentration at time t, m (mg) is the adsorbent mass, and V (mL) is solution volume.

According to the literature, MgAl-LDH synthesized via the hydrothermal synthesis method has a uniformly tailored hexagonal plate structure and could strongly adsorb anionic compounds (Sohrabnezhad et al., 2020). Thus, MgAl-LDH was chosen herein as the substrate for loading SDBS. The two-step procedure for synthesizing CDs@MgAl-LDO is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1a. First, the SDBS-decorated MgAl-LDHs were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method at 150 °C in the presence of SDBS by using magnesium nitrate and aluminum nitrate as the metal sources and urea as the alkali source. Second, the synthesized SDBS@MgAl-LDH precursor was pyrolyzed under N2 flow to form a CDs@MgAl-LDO product. The negative nature of SDBS are crucial to the formation of the final LDO nanocomposite coated with CDs. The zeta potentials of MgAl-LDH indicated its positive nature in a weakly alkaline solution (Fig. S1). Therefore, SDBS was first fastened onto the MgAl-LDH surface because of electrostatic interactions between positively charged LDHs and anionic surfactant molecules during the hydrothermal procedure. After pyrolysis, water escaped from LDH sheets to form LDOs, and well-dispersed CDs that had been generated by the transformation of SDBS were grown on MgAl-LDO sheets in situ.

The properties of the CDs on the MgAl-LDO sheets were controlled by the initial addition of SDBS and the pyrolysis conditions. The CDs@MgAl-LDO hybrid synthesized using 15 mmol of SDBS as the carbon source had the highest dye uptake for three dyes (Fig. 1b). By comparison, MgAl-LDO (without SDBS) had a low dye uptake, especially for two anionic dyes. Thus, the modified CDs on LODs played a crucial role in improving the adsorption capacity of CDs@MgAl-LDO. The CDs@MgAl-LDO composite made by calcinating the SDBS@MgAl-LDH precursor at 450 °C under N2 flow exhibited the best adsorption performance for both cationic and anionic dyes (Fig. 1c). Conversely, the CDs@MgAl-LDO450M composite made by the pyrolysis of the SDBS@MgAl-LDH precursor under air flow displayed a considerably low adsorption capacity for the three dyes, because carbon escaped in the form of CO2. The effects of calcination conditions on the crystallinity and particle size of CDs@MgAl-LDO were evaluated via XRD and by using a Malvern Laster particle size analyzer. XRD pattern of the products obtained at various pyrolysis temperature indicated that the CDs@MgAl-LDO prepared by the pyrolysis of SDBS@MgAl-LDH at 450 °C and 550 °C exhibited the strong characteristic diffraction peaks of LDOs (Fig. S2a). Additionally, the products after calcination had a slightly larger average size than SDBS@MgAl-LDH (Fig. S2b), a feature that may be ascribed to the increase in the hydrophobicity of the adsorbents that led to particle agglomeration. Eventually, CDs@MgAl-LDO was prepared under the optimized conditions and used to adsorb the three dyes in water solutions.

Owing to the interlayer ion-exchange property of hydrotalcites, some small organic anions could be exchanged into their interspaces, which would inevitably affect the crystalline structure of host hydroxide sheets. Thus, MgAl-LDH, SDBS@MgAl-LDH and CDs@MgAl-LDO were characterized via XRD to monitor the changes in their crystalline structure. MgAl-LDH had five characteristic diffraction peaks at 11.7°, 23.5°, 34.8°, 60.9° and 62.2° ( Fig. 2a), which were assigned to the (003), (006), (012), (110), and (113) planes of hydroxide, respectively (Duman et al., 2016). Compared with those of MgAl-LDH, the main diffraction peaks of SDBS@MgAl-LDH evidently decreased because the CDs obstructed the crystal plane of hydroxide; nevertheless, their positions remained almost the same. The basal spacing (d) of MgAl-LDH and SDBS@MgAl-LDH composites were calculated from the (003) plane by using Bragg’s equation. Accordingly, the layer distance for the LDH layers of both MgAl-LDH and SDBS@MgAl-LDH was set at 0.28 nm (Sohrabnezhad et al., 2020). These results indicated that no SDBS intercalation occurred during the hydrothermal process, and the crystalline structure of hydroxide was maintained well (Zhang et al., 2017). The diffraction peaks of MgO and MgAl2O4 were positioned at 43.1° and 62.5°, respectively, indicating that the crystal transformation from LDHs to LDOs occurred (Zhao et al., 2013).

The FTIR spectra of MgAl-LDH, SDBS@MgAl-LDH and CDs@MgAl-LDO are shown in Fig. 2b. The typical absorption peaks at the low wavenumber region of 400–1000 cm−1 were related to the stretching and bending vibrations of metal–oxygen (Mg–O and Al–O) groups in the three composites. Compared with pure MgAl-LDH, SDBS@MgAl-LDH displayed new absorption peaks at 2939 and 1201 cm−1, which were attributed to the vibrations of C–H and SO, respectively, suggesting that SDBS attached onto the LDHs. After pyrolysis, CDs@MgAl-LDO showed new characteristic absorption peaks at 1419 and 1129 cm−1, which were attributed to CC and C–O groups, respectively.

The Raman spectrum of CDs@MgAl-LDO shows three characteristics bands of carbon material, G band at 1595 cm−1, a board D band at 1352 cm−1, and an apparent 2D band at around 2705 cm−1. The coexistence of both G and 2D bands suggested that the CDs on the surface of LDOs were graphitic structure (Vatankhah et al., 2019).

The specific surface area and the porous structures of CDs@MgAl-LDO and MgAl-LDO were analyzed via N2 adsorption-desorption measurements. Both products exhibited type IV isotherms with H4 type hysteresis loops (Fig. 2c), revealing the coexistence of both micropores and mesopores. The BJH total pore volume of CDs@MgAl-LDO and MgAl-LDO was 0.094 and 0.023 cm3/g, respectively. CDs@MgAl-LDO had a higher BET specific surface area (SBET = 28.61 m2/g) than MgAl-LDO (SBET =11.48 m2/g).

The XPS spectra of CDs@MgAl-LDO were recorded to investigate the element states on its surface. The peaks of Mg, Al, O, and C elements were observed in the full XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S4). The C1s spectrum of CDs@MgAl-LDO (Fig. 2d) was split into three peaks at 284.8, 286.6, and 289.1 eV for C–C/CC, C–O, and O–CO groups, respectively. The O1s spectrum was split into two peaks at 529.8 and 531.7 eV for metal–oxygen and C–O groups, respectively.

Static contact angle test with water droplets was conducted to evaluate the hydrophilicity of CDs@MgAl-LDO. For comparison, the hydrophilicity of MgAl-LDO was also evaluated. According to a previous study (Lin et al., 2014), hydrotalcite composites are highly hydrophilic owing to abundant hydroxyl groups on their surface. As expected, MgAl-LDO clearly showed an intrinsically hydrophilic surface with a static contact angle of 11.5° (Fig. 2f), indicating that it had good wettability. After incorporating the CDs, the static contact angle of CDs@MgAl-LDO changed to 49.8°. The increase in hydrophobicity could help to improve the affinity of the composites for organic contaminants. These results indicated that CDs@MgAl-LDO is a versatile adsorption material with both hydrophilic hydroxyl groups and hydrophobic CDs, which aid in capturing target organic contaminants via hydrophobic interactions while keeping good dispersion in water solutions.

SEM images of MgAl-LDO and CDs@MgAl-LDO are shown in Fig. 3a–d. Both pure LDO and the as-synthesized CDs@MgAl-LDO possessed uniform hexagonal lamellar architectures. CDs@MgAl-LDO had a plate lateral size of approximately 500 nm and a thickness of 42 nm, which was considerably smaller than that of MgAl-LDO. These results suggested that the existence of SDBS in the precursor solution did not change the hexagonal lamellar structure but substantially reduced the size of LDH sheets. Furthermore, CDs@MgAl-LDO had a lighter color, which was caused by coating the CDs on the surface of LDOs. EDS and elemental mapping were performed to characterize the elemental contents of CDs@MgAl-LDO and their spatial distributions. CDs@MgAl-LDO mainly contained C, Mg, Al, and O elements (Fig. 3e and f), in which C was present in 16.8% by weight. C mapping showed good distribution without any aggregation. These results also indicated that SDBS was uniformly anchored on the surface of LDHs during the hydrothermal process and eventually changed to well-dispersed CDs after calcination treatment. HRTEM images of CDs@MgAl-LDO further indicated that it had the typical hexagonal lamellar structure that contained horizontal and standing nanosheets (Fig. 3g). Moreover, CDs@MgAl-LDO was composed of an LDO matrix (lattice fringe of 0.25 nm) (Song et al., 2021) decorated with spherical graphitic CDs (lattice distance of 0.16 nm) with a diameter of approximately 3.3 nm (see Fig. 3h) (Wu et al., 2021).

The influence of adsorption time on the adsorption of MB, DB5, and CR by CDs@MgAl-LDO at 25 °C was investigated. The amount of the three dyes with a concentration of 150 and 200 mg/L adsorbed by CDs@MgAl-LDO strongly depended on the adsorption time ( Fig. 4a). Moreover, CDs@MgAl-LDO showed supernormal adsorption performance for all dyes. The adsorption data were then fitted by pseudo-first-order (PF) and pseudo-second-order (PS) kinetic models and particle internal diffusion models:
(2)
log
(
q
1 e
−
q
t
)
=
log
q
1 e
−
K
1
2.303
t
(3)
t
q
t
=
1
K
2
q
2 e
2
+
t
q
2 e
,
(4)
q
t
=
k
p
t
1 2
+ C ,
where q
t and q
1e and q
2e (mg/g) are the adsorption capacity of CDs@MgAl-LDO to the dyes at time t and equilibrium time, respectively; K
1 and K
2 are kinetic rate constants; k
p is the rate constant of intraparticle diffusion (mg/g/min−1/2), which is used to compare mass transfer rates; and C (mg/g) is a constant related to boundary layer thickness.

As shown in Table S1 and Fig. 4b, PS model exhibited a good linearity with the correlation coefficient (R2) between 0.9901 and 0.9911, which was higher than that of the PF model. Moreover, the q
1e value calculated from the PS model was very close to the experimental data, indicating that the adsorption capacity of CDs@MgAl-LDO to the three dyes was more consistent with the PS model than with the PF model. Notably, linear fitting of the intraparticle diffusion model for dye adsorption presented multiple linearity that involved three steps (Fig. 4c). The sharp initial region with strongly increasing adsorption capacity was the instantaneous diffusion stage or the surface adsorption stage because of the presence of numerous active sites that were unsaturated on the adsorbent surface and the existence of large amounts of dye that were rapidly transported onto the adsorbent. In the second step, the adsorption rates obviously decreased. This step was labeled as the gradual or slow adsorption stage, which was always the rate-limiting stage. In the third step, diffusion resistance increased because of adsorption saturation of the external surface of the adsorbent, leading to a sharp decrease in diffusion rate. Moreover, the increase in adsorption capacity was terminated by a saturation plateau. The intercept C values were non-zero, indicating that adsorption behaviors involved complex mechanisms, including both physical and chemical adsorption (Hu et al., 2021b).

The influence of initial dye concentration (200–400 mg/L) on adsorption was evaluated via batch adsorption tests with CDs@MgAl-LDO as the adsorbent. The adsorption capacity of MgAl-LDO and SDBS@MgAl-LDH to the three dyes at the said concentrations was also tested for comparison. As depicted in Fig. S5, the uptake of the dyes initially increased as their concentration increased and then gradually reached equilibrium. The adsorption mechanism was further explored by fitting the adsorption data by using the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models (Table S2). The adsorption data evidently fitted the Freundlich isotherm model better than the Langmuir isotherm model, indicating that heterogeneous adsorption occurred. Various functional groups were distributed on the surface of CDs@MgAl-LDO, which resulted from the CDs and LDOs that provided different active sites for the dyes. Thus, the dyes were adsorbed on a heterogeneous surface. Nevertheless, the Langmuir model also gave a decent correlation coefficient with values above 0.90. These results demonstrated that the adsorption of the three dyes by CDs@MgAl-LDO included both physical and chemical mechanisms (Gao et al., 2019). Additionally, the equilibrium parameter (RL) values gained from the Langmuir isotherm were between 0.006 and 0.074 (RL = 0, irreversible; 0 < RL < 1, favorable; RL > 1, unfavorable); this marks that the adsorption of the three dyes on CDs@MgAl-LDO were favorable. The maximum adsorption capacity for the three dyes as calculated from the Langmuir isotherm model ranged from 3628.9 mg/g to 5174.1 mg/g, which was substantially higher than that of MgAl-LDO (Table S3) and SDBS@MgAl-LDH (Table S4), illustrating the important role of CDs. Obviously, CDs@MgAl-LDO showed a higher adsorption capacity for MG than that of CR and DB5. That might be because that MG was a typical triphenylmethane dye, possessing more available hydrophobic sites for bonding CDs@MgAl-LDO via hydrophobic interactions; but the existing of hydrophilia SO3 − groups in the CR and DB5 molecules made them easy to be dispersed in water, and thus led to a lower dye uptake (Lyu et al., 2020). The adsorption performance of CDs@MgAl-LDO was also compared with the recently reported LDH-based adsorbents. It was found that CDs@MgAl-LDO exhibited a much higher adsorption capacity than the adsorbents listed in Table 1. Furthermore, the presence of heavy metals in adsorbents could bring certain environmental problems. Thus, CDs@MgAl-LDO was much environmentally friendly than the LDH/LDO composites containing Cu, Co, Mn and Ni.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH and adsorption mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of solutions can influence the surface charge of adsorption phase and adsorbed molecules. Thus, the adsorption properties of the adsorbents were investigated within the pH range of 5.0–10.0 to keep the hydroxyl layers intact and to prevent MG precipitation in alkaline solutions (Daud et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 5a, CDs@MgAl-LDO exhibited excellent adsorption ability for MG within the indicated pH range, and the change in solution pH almost did not affect its adsorption performance. The uptake of anionic dyes by CDs@MgAl-LDO decreased as pH increased.

The mechanisms by which CDs@MgAl-LDO adsorbs the three dyes are illustrated in Fig. 6. MG is a typical cationic dye that is protonated and positively charged under acidic conditions. The results of zeta potential measurements indicated that CDs@MgAl-LDO was positively charged within pH 3.0–9.5 (Fig. 5b). Therefore, electrostatic interaction was not the main factor that influenced the adsorption of MG by CDs@MgAl-LDO but rather some other mechanisms. CDs@MgAl-LDO possessed uniformly distributed hydrophobic binding sites transformed from SDBS that could interact with organic contaminants through hydrophobic interactions. Thus, hydrophobic interaction could be contributed to the total adsorption mechanisms. This conclusion was proved by the isothermal adsorption experiments. As it indicated in Table S3, the adsorption capacity of CDs@MgAl-LDO after modified with CDs was much higher than that of MgAl-LDO itself, indicating the hydrophobic CDs on the surface of LDOs were favorable for more dye adsorption. In addition, surface complexation might exist between the metals of CDs@MgAl-LDO and the supplying groups of the dye molecules. This hypothesis was proved by comparing the FTIR spectra of CDs@MgAl-LDO before and after MG adsorption. From Fig. S6, a slight shift from 449 to 443 cm–1 was observed for the characteristic band of metal–oxygen (Mg–O) in dye-adsorbed CDs@MgAl-LDO compared to that of CDs@MgAl-LDO, suggesting that surface complexation occurred in the adsorption. Additionally, according to previous reports, the free hydroxyl groups of CDs introduced on the LDH surface form hydrogen bonds with adsorbates improves adsorption capacity(Zhang et al., 2014). Therefore, the adsorption of MG by CDs@MgAl-LDO could be controlled by hydrophobic interactions, surface complexation, and/or hydrogen-bonds.

Electrostatic attraction plays a vital role in the adsorption of DB5 and CR owing to the tendency of dye uptake to decline as pH increases. At low pH values, given that CDs@MgAl-LDO had more positive charges, it could likely adsorb CR and CB (which are permanently negatively charged over a wide pH range) because of the improvement in electrostatic attraction. As pH increased, the electrostatic attraction between CDs@MgAl-LDO and the dyes gradually decreased and led to a decrease in dye uptake. Furthermore, CDs@MgAl-LDO still exhibited a good adsorption performance with dye uptake of over 1337.1 mg/g at pH 10.0 as both the adsorbent and the two dyes were negatively charged. This result indicated that hydrophobic interaction, surface complexation, and/or hydrogen bonding also affected cationic dye adsorption.

The adsorption mechanism was also elucidated via XRD analysis. LDOs can reconstruct their initial LDH structures by spontaneous rehydration and exchanging anions into the interlayer space when dispersed in water (Jia and Liu, 2019). The XRD patterns of CDs@MgAl-LDO after adsorbing the three dyes are shown in Fig. 5c. The CDs@MgAl-LDO composites loaded with the three dyes all exhibited the characteristic XRD diffractions of the initial LDHs, suggesting that CDs@MgAl-LDO maintained the “memory effects” of LDOs after it was coated with CDs. Moreover, the interlayer space of CDs@MgAl-LDO did not change after adsorbing MG, DB5, and CR, indicating that the dyes were adsorbed on the adsorbent’s surface, and ion exchange did not occur during the adsorption of both cationic and anionic dyes.

In view of the low stability of the LDH/LDO composites in acid solutions, water, ethanol, NaOH (5%), ethanol-NaOH (5%) (v/v, 9:1) were selected as recycling reagent. The adsorption-desorption cycles were conducted by using 0.01 g of CDs@MgAl-LDO dispersing in 200 mg/L of MG, CR, and DB5 solutions (30 mL). As shown in Fig. S7a, the adsorbed dyes were easily eluted by using 10 mL of ethanol in two steps (5 +5 mL). Ethanol is a low-polar solvent compared with water and NaOH solution. Therefore, it could be concluded that hydrophobic interactions played vital roles in the adsorption. The recycling experiments shown in Fig. S7b suggest that the dye removal percentage of CDs@MgAl-LDO kept at 90% for MG after 8 cycles, 60% for CR after 4 cycles, 51% for DB5 after 4cycles, suggesting that the CDs@MgAl-LDO composite possessed good reusability for cationic dyes.

This study developed a facile approach for preparing a novel LDO hybrid coated with CDs. This hybrid material was achieved via in situ transformation of SDBS into CDs on the LDH surface by carbonization. CDs@MgAl-LDO had an improved SBET of 28.61 m2/g and displayed a hexagonal lamellar nanostructure. EDS analysis and elemental mapping revealed that the CDs were dispersed well on the LDO surface with a carbon content of up to 16.8%. Owing to the hydrophilic nature of LDOs and the modified hydrophobic CDs, CDs@MgAl-LDO exhibited superior adsorption capacity for MG, DB5, and CR while keeping good dispersity in water. The maximum adsorption capacity of CDs@MgAl-LDO as calculated from the Langmuir isotherm model in relation to MG, DB5, and CR ranged from 3628.9 mg/g to 5174.1 mg/g. The facile approach presented herein provides an effective method for constructing carbon/LDO hybrids for printing and dyeing wastewater treatment in place of active carbon and other commercial materials.

Jiahui Ge: the contributions to the adsorption and characterization experiments, and original draft preparation. Lili Lian: the contributions to the design of the work, writing-review & editing, and project administration. Xiyue Wang: the contributions to the FTIR and XRD analysis. Xueling Cao: the contributions to the Fluorescence spectrum and BET analysis. Wenxiu Gao: the contributions to the mechanism analysis. Dawei Lou: the contributions to editing and project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work is supported by the projects of Science and Technology Development of Jilin Province (Nos. 20210509063RQ, 20190303116SF and 202002008JC), the Research and Development Project for Industrial Technology of Jilin Province (No. 2020C028-1), and the Science Foundation of Distinguished Young Scholars of Jilin City (No. 20190104158). Financial support from the Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals of Jilin Province is also acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.127613.

.
